title: Cleaning and Visualizations
text:
Row Filtering: Removed non-participant rows (e.g., “Mean”, “SD”, summary labels) to ensure only valid observations were included.
Data Joining: Merged two data sets using participant IDs to combine behavioral and demographic information.
Column Selection: Dropped unnecessary columns (e.g., extra “x#” variables and personal identifiers) and retained only relevant variables up to delta_building_test.

title: Cleaning and Visualizations
text:
Row Filtering: Removed non-participant rows (e.g., “Mean”, “SD”, summary labels) to ensure only valid observations were included.
Data Joining: Merged two data sets using participant IDs to combine behavioral and demographic information.
Column Selection: Dropped unnecessary columns (e.g., extra “x#” variables and personal identifiers) and retained only relevant variables up to delta_building_test.

title: Cleaning and Visualizations
text:
Visualization 1
This visualization shows how manual dexterity scores relate to simple reaction time. Each point represents one participant. The horizontal axis shows the participant’s average simple reaction time, while the vertical axis shows their Building Test score, which measures manual dexterity. Participants are separated by sex using different colors and shapes. A trend line is shown for each sex group to make it easier to compare whether the relationship between reaction time and dexterity looks similar or different across groups.





Manual dexterity scores by simple reaction time and sex.

title: Cleaning and Visualizations
text:
Visualization 2
This visualization compares how manual dexterity scores changed from the practice test to the actual test for each sex group. The horizontal axis shows the difference between a person’s practice and actual Building Test scores, while the vertical axis separates participants by sex. The shape of each group shows how spread out the score differences are. Wider areas mean more participants had score differences in that range. The box in the middle shows the typical range of score differences for each group. Overall, this visualization helps compare whether one sex group tended to have larger changes in dexterity performance or more variation in scores than the other.





Score of practice to actual manual dexterity scores by sex.

title: Kiwi Cervantes
text: Kiwi Cervantes is a Graduating Kinesiology student at California State University, Monterey Bay. They have just gotten into their desired Graduate Program for their M.S. In Kinesiology at San Francisco State University.

title: Kiwi Cervantes
text:
Education
California State Monterey Bay | Marina, CA
B.S. in Kinesiology | 2023 - 2026

title: Kiwi Cervantes
text:
Experience
CSUMB Cooperative Learning Center | Anatomy & Physiology Tutor | August 2025 - present

title: About
text:
Hi, my name is Kiwi Cervantes. I am a senior Kinesiology major at California State University, Monterey Bay with a concentration in Exercise Science and a minor in Statistics. I am also an ACSM Certified Exercise Physiologist and a CRLA Level 2 Certified Advanced Tutor.
My academic interests connect exercise science, statistics, and research. Through my Statistics minor, I have developed skills in data visualization, statistical computing with R, nonparametric statistics, and generalized linear models. I also work as a Statistics Tutor and Anatomy and Physiology Mentor Tutor.
I am currently involved in undergraduate research through CSUMB’s Undergraduate Research Opportunity Center as a Koret Scholar. My research experience includes assisting with a study on esports athletes by collecting dexterity, cognitive, and cardiovascular data. This project has helped me apply my interests in kinesiology, statistics, and data analysis to real research questions.

title: The Data
text:
Identify your data source.

Observational collected data from local Monterey and CSUMB Esports tournaments, CSUMB library, and other non-random methods. Subjects voluntarily participated in a Purdue Pegboard test to measure dexterity, Psytool kit tests to assess simple reaction time, Go/No go test for impulsivity, and Poesner Cueing test for spatial orientation. Additionally, participants responded to a questionnaire describing hours of sleep per week, caffeine intake, and other items of interest.

Describe your data, including variables and data types.

Health and Lifestyle: Sex - Male or Female
Age_years Gaming_hr_week_ratio - Hours per week gaming Caffeine_Intake_mg_d - Reported caffeine intake (mg) per day Sleep_hrs - Typical hours of sleep per night Exercise_hours_week - Hours per week exercising Difference (Actual mean - Practice mean) of Cognitive measure test in milliseconds (ms): SR_ROC_Mean_RT - Simple Reaction test ROC_Mean_Go_RT - Go/No Go test ROC_Mean_Valid_Cue_RT_ms - Poesner Cueing test (valid cue) PP_ROC_Building_Test - Purdue Pegboard test Level of Gaming/Type: Gaming (measured as Non-Gamer, Casual-SB, Elite-SB, FPS, MOBA) Abbreviations: SB (Super Smash Bros Ultimate), FPS (First Person Shooter), MOBA (multiplayer online battle arena)

Identify the research questions you want to answer.

Is manual dexterity related to simple reaction time? Does the relationship between manual dexterity and simple reaction time differ by sex? Does the difference between practice and actual manual dexterity scores differ by sex?
